:::{#def-first-order-interval-fail-prob}
#### First-order interval failure probability

To first order in $\Delta t$, the
[interval failure probability](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#def-interval-fail-prob) $q_j(\tau)$
is the hazard times the interval width.
We give that approximation its own symbol, the **first-order interval failure probability**:

$$
q^*_j(\tau) \eqdef \haz(\tau)\,\Delta t,
$$

so that

$$
q_j(\tau) \approx q^*_j(\tau).
$$
:::

:::{#exm-first-order-interval-fail-prob}
#### First-order approximation versus the exact value

Continue the setup of the
[interval failure probability example](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#exm-interval-fail-prob):
subject $j$ has constant hazard $\haz(t) = 0.1$ per year,
on the half-year interval $[4.0,\, 4.5)$ with $\Delta t = 0.5$ years.
The first-order approximation replaces the exact value with the hazard times the interval width:

$$
\ba
q^*_j(4.0)
&= \haz(4.0)\,\Delta t
&& \text{(definition of } q^*_j \text{)}
\\
&= 0.1 \times 0.5
&& \text{(substitute } \haz = 0.1,\ \Delta t = 0.5\text{)}
\\
&= 0.05 .
&& \text{(arithmetic)}
\ea
$$

The first-order approximation $q^*_j(4.0) = 0.05$ overstates the exact value $q_j(4.0) \approx 0.0488$
by about $0.0012$, a relative error of roughly $2.5\%$.
The discrepancy is exactly the leading term dropped by the linearization
$1 - \expf{-x} = x - \frac{x^2}{2} + \cdots$:
here $x = \haz(4.0)\,\Delta t = 0.05$,
so the dropped term is $\frac{x^2}{2} = \frac{0.05^2}{2} \approx 0.00125$,
which shrinks to zero as $\Delta t \to 0$.
This vanishing discrepancy is why proofs based on this discretization become exact in the continuous-time limit.
:::
